sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
seg = current_frame.segmentation
print("All non-background nodes:")
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] != 'S':
        print(f"  id={n['id']}, color={n['color']}, pixels={n['pixels']}, boundary={n['boundary'][:6]}")

# Count green pixels
green_total = sum(n['pixels'] for n in seg['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'N')
print(f"\nTotal green pixels: {green_total}")

# Check ASCII
ascii_lines = current_frame.ascii.split('\n')
print("\n=== Current state ===")
for i in range(35, 55):
    print(f"Row {i}: {ascii_lines[i][30:55]}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.content:
I see the situation now. There are still green pixels remaining:
1. A green shape at rows 29-34 (above the white shape) - 45 pixels
2. Two green 3x3 blocks inside the white shape's hollow area at rows 38-40 - 18 pixels
3. One green 3x3 block inside the white shape's lower hollow area at rows 47-49 - 9 pixels
Total: 72 green pixels. The original green frame had 144 pixels. So about half has been absorbed.
Looking at the image, there's a green block floating above the white shape. The white shape has green blocks inside its hollow center. 
I think the goal might be to position the white shape so that it "catches" all the green pieces inside its hollow areas. The green block above needs to be caught too.
Let me think about this differently. The white shape has a specific structure with hollow areas. When it overlaps with the green frame, the green parts that are NOT inside the hollow areas get absorbed/destroyed, while the green parts that ARE inside the hollow areas remain visible.
So the goal might be to position the white shape so that ALL green pixels end up inside the hollow areas of the white shape. Currently there's a green block above the white shape that hasn't been caught yet.
Let me try moving UP to catch that green block above.

Plan: Move UP to catch the green block above, then adjust position to collect all remaining green.
